purpose: Provides rigorous quantitative framework for determining whether credit spreads adequately compensate for risk by decomposing into expected loss, risk premium, and residual components.
components: Expected loss calculation from addback-adjusted default probabilities
Composition adjustment for index quality migration (BB 38% to 52%)
Residual risk premium vs post-2000 distribution (currently 0-80bp, median 180-220bp)
Cross-validation against Merton-model equity-implied spreads (20-40bp wider than actual)
Central bank put adjustment (~30-50bp structural compression)
current_assessment: Net mispricing after composition adjustment is 30-80bp rather than 100-150bp. Bottom-decile residual risk premium confirmed but partially explained by composition improvement and central bank backstop. Called single most actionable insight by pair_1 debate.
limitations: Addback adjustment introduces uncertainty propagating through all downstream calculations
Central bank put magnitude confounded by multiple factors
65bp estimation error range means residual compression may not be statistically significant
Model-dependent decomposition sensitive to default probability assumptions
